These questions include the most important thing in life, which depends on one's situation (like food for someone starving, warmth for someone cold, etc.). However, once basic needs are met, the author suggests there's another need—figuring out who we are and why we are here. The author emphasizes that this isn't a casual interest but a fundamental question that's part of a long-standing debate in human history, more important than things like Olympic medals. The author is saying there is something that does interest everyone, so A is probably not the right answer. (B) Different people may have different interests and concerns. However, the main point seems to go beyond that to argue that despite these differences, there is a common concern. This seems to be the central idea the author is making towards the end, so this might be the correct answer. (D) People in modern society pay more attention to philosophical questions.
